title: Third-order intrinsic anomalous Hall effect as a transport fingerprint of altermagnets
authors: Longjun Xiang, Hao Jin, Jian Wang
abstract: The intrinsic anomalous Hall effect (IAHE) provides a powerful transport fingerprint of quantum magnets, with its linear and second-order responses distinguishing ferromagnets and $\mathcal{P}\mathcal{T}$-symmetric antiferromagnets, respectively. Altermagnets, as an emergent class of quantum magnets, have recently been shown to host a third-order extrinsic anomalous Hall effect, raising a question of whether an \textit{intrinsic} counterpart can serve as a diagnostic of altermagnetic order. Based on spin-group symmetry analysis, we demonstrate that the third-order IAHE is generically allowed in the ten spin Laue groups relevant to altermagnets when spin-orbit coupling (SOC) is taken into account. By combining these symmetry constraints with the anomalous velocity induced by the second-order Berry curvature, we uncover a resonant third-order IAHE arising near the altermagnetic band crossings at generic momenta in both the Lieb-lattice altermagnet and the experimentally realized altermagnet V$_2$Se$_2$O. Notably, we identify the Berry curvature quadrupole, encoded in the second-order Berry curvature and activated by finite SOC, as the microscopic quantum geometric origin of this resonance. Our results establish the third-order IAHE as an intrinsic quantum geometric transport fingerprint of altermagnets and extend the hierarchy of IAHE across collinear quantum magnets.
summary: This paper identifies the third-order intrinsic anomalous Hall effect as a unique transport fingerprint for altermagnets, distinguishing them from ferromagnets and antiferromagnets. By using symmetry analysis and quantum geometry, the authors show that this effect arises from the Berry curvature quadrupole. They demonstrate a resonant enhancement of the effect near altermagnetic band crossings and suggest it is experimentally detectable in materials like V2Se2O. This work establishes a new hierarchy of Hall effects across different classes of collinear quantum magnets.
main_problem: Determining whether an intrinsic counterpart to the third-order extrinsic anomalous Hall effect exists to serve as a diagnostic transport fingerprint for altermagnets.
main_result: The study demonstrates that the third-order intrinsic anomalous Hall effect (IAHE) is generically allowed in altermagnetic spin Laue groups and identifies the Berry curvature quadrupole as its microscopic quantum geometric origin.
method: The authors use spin-group symmetry analysis, semiclassical theory, and quantum-geometric decomposition of the conductivity tensor into Berry curvature and quantum metric contributions.
model_or_system: The study focuses on altermagnets, specifically utilizing a 2D Lieb-lattice model and the experimentally realized van der Waals altermagnet V2Se2O.
key_observables: Third-order intrinsic anomalous Hall conductivity, Hall voltage, Berry curvature quadrupole (BCQ), and acceleration quantum metric dipole (AQMD).
important_parameters: Spin-orbit coupling (SOC) strength, chemical potential near band crossings, and the moderately dirty regime (scattering time ~ 10^-14 s).
main_assumptions: The analysis focuses on the intrinsic nature of the effect and assumes a moderately dirty metallic regime; it does not discuss the T-even third-order extrinsic effect.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the hierarchy of IAHE across FM, AFM, and AM; Figure 2 displays the Lieb-lattice structure and band dispersions; Figure 4 shows band dispersions with spin-flip SOC and the quantum-geometric decomposition of conductivity.
paper_structure: The paper begins by establishing the hierarchy of IAHE, moves to symmetry analysis of the third-order IAHE tensor, provides a quantum-geometric decomposition of the effect, analyzes specific models (Lieb-lattice and V2Se2O), and concludes with experimental feasibility estimates.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The hierarchy of IAHE across collinear quantum magnets. (a) The ferromagnet (FM) with a spin-polarized Fermi-surface can be identified by the linear IAHE. (b) The PT -symmetric antiferromag- net (AFM) with a spin degenerate Fermi surface can be identified by the second-order IAHE. (c) The altermagnet (AM) with an alterna- tive spin-splitting Fermi surface can be identified by the third-order IAHE (This work). Note that the opposite-spin sublattices in AFM are connected with PT or T τ1/2 symmetry, while the opposite-spin sublattices in AM are connected with CnT or MaT symmetry2.

FIG. 2. Third-order IAHE in 2D Lieb-lattice altermagnet. (a) The 2D Lieb-lattice. Here the blue dashed square labels the unit cell. (b) The spin-polarized band dispersions of Eq. (4) with λz = 0. (c) The band dispersions of Eq. (4) with λz = 0.01t1. Here the colorbar denotes the expectation value of σz. (d) The dependence of σ(0/2) yxxx and σ(0/2) yxyy on the chemical potential µ. (e) The quantum- geometric decompositions of σ(0) yxxx. (f) The normalized k-resolved integrand (contributed by BCQ) for the resonant peak µ1 of σ(0) yxxx. Parameters: 4t1 = −0.4eV, 2t2 = +0.025eV, 2td = +0.125eV, J = −0.165eV, T = 100K, and τ = 10−14s.

FIG. 3. Third-order IAHE in van der Waals altermagnet V2Se2O. (a) and (b): The band dispersions of V2Se2O2 without and with SOC, respectively. (c) The dependence of σ(0/2) yxxx and σ(0/2) yxyy on the chem- ical potential µ. To evaluate σ(2) abcd, we choose τ = 10−14s. (d) The quantum-geometric decomposition of σ(0) yxxx. (e) and (f): The nor- malized k-resolved integrand (contributed by BCQ) for the µ1 and µ2 resonant peaks of σ(0) yxxx.

FIG. 4. Third-order IAHE in 2D Lieb-lattice altermagnets with a spin-flip SOC. (a) The band dispersions of Eq. (4) with an additional spin-flip SOC (λ∥= 0.2t1). (b) The quantum-geometric decompo- sitions of σ(0) yxxx. (c-d) The normalized k-resolved integrand (con- tributed by AQMD) for σ(0) yxxx for the µ1 and µ2 peaks, respectively.
